title: Multi-Agent Systems: When More Than One AI Worker Is Involved
thesis: Multi-agent systems split complex work across specialized AI agents, but the engineering value comes from coordination, communication, evaluation, and cost tradeoffs, not from simply adding more agents.

claims.claim: A multi-agent system solves a problem by dividing it among specialized agents and coordinating their work, not by simply running several models in parallel.

evidence.snippet: A multi-agent system is one composed of multiple interacting, autonomous agents, where the key problem is coordinating those agents so that they can work together.

counterevidence.summary: Some implementations call themselves multi-agent while mostly running the same prompt across several outputs and picking the best one, which is closer to ensembling than true division of labor.

claims.claim: Multi-agent systems borrow ideas from distributed systems, ensemble methods, and organizational design, not just recent AI research.

evidence.snippet: Research in multi-agent systems draws on ideas from economics, game theory, and distributed computing, treating coordination as a central problem.

counterevidence.summary: Classical multi-agent work often assumes formally specified agents and environments, whereas LLM-based agents are defined informally by prompts, making direct transfer of older theory difficult.

claims.claim: Common multi-agent patterns include sequential pipelines, manager-and-workers, and debate-and-review, and each pattern carries different coordination risks.

evidence.snippet: Conversational programming with multiple agents enables flexible patterns such as sequential chats, group chats, and nested chats for task decomposition.
Multiple instances of a language model debate their answers over several rounds, improving reasoning and factual accuracy.

counterevidence.summary: These patterns are not standardized; different frameworks implement them with different interfaces, error-handling rules, and observability guarantees.

claims.claim: Adding agents increases coordination cost, ambiguity, and failure modes; a multi-agent design should be justified by a specific division of labor, not by default.

evidence.snippet: Debate improves accuracy on some reasoning tasks but increases the number of model calls, latency, and the complexity of aggregating conflicting outputs.

counterevidence.summary: For some simple tasks, a carefully written single-agent prompt can match or beat a multi-agent setup at lower cost and latency.

sources.title: Wooldridge: An Introduction to MultiAgent Systems
Du, Li, Tarlow, et al.: Improving Factuality and Reasoning in Language Models through Multiagent Debate
ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
Wu, Bansal, Jin, et al.: AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit.
Treat maturity=seed as an explicit uncertainty marker.
Do not present multi-agent systems as automatically better than single-agent systems.
When summarizing, preserve the plain-language-first, technical-depth-later structure.

reviews.notes: Sibling-agent review against article-proposal-ideation eval-card. Privacy scan passed. No proprietary or personal content detected.
Human author approved the draft for publication.
